questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests understanding of open/closed principles, plugin registries, and runtime configurability for cross-cutting concerns like logging.

## Key Concepts
- Strategy pattern
- Registry / plugin architecture
- Thread-safety and dynamic configuration
- Separation of concerns in logging

## Code Example
```javascript
interface LogEvent { level: string; message: string; ts: number; }
interface LogFormatter { format(event: LogEvent): string; }
class JSONFormatter implements LogFormatter { format(e: LogEvent) { return JSON.stringify(e); } }
class KVFormatter implements LogFormatter { format(e: LogEvent) { return `ts=${e.ts} level=${e.level} msg='${e.message}'`; } }
class Registry<T> { private map = new Map<string, T>(); register(name: string, v: T){ this.map.set(name, v); } get(name: string): T | undefined { return this.map.get(name); } }
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you handle thread-safety in a multi-threaded environment?
- How to test registering and switching formatters in isolation?

## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to design extensible, runtime-extendable components with minimal coupling using Strategy + Registry patterns.

## Key Concepts
- Strategy: encapsulate evaluation logic per flag
- Registry/Plugin: dynamic, runtime-extensible factories
- Dependency Injection: decouples engine from evaluators
- Thread-safety and versioning considerations for runtime extensions

## Code Example
```typescript
interface Context { tenantId: string; userId: string; region?: string; [key: string]: any }
interface Evaluator { evaluate(ctx: Context): boolean }
interface EvaluatorFactory { create(): Evaluator }
class Registry { private map = new Map<string, EvaluatorFactory>(); register(n: string, f: EvaluatorFactory) { this.map.set(n, f) } getFactory(n: string): EvaluatorFactory | undefined { return this.map.get(n) } }
class FlagEngine { constructor(private registry: Registry) {} isEnabled(flag: string, ctx: Context): boolean { const f = this.registry.getFactory(flag); if (!f) throw new Error(`Unknown flag: ${flag}`); return f.create().evaluate(ctx); } }
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you handle hot-reload of strategies without restarting the service?
- How would you version/evolve evaluator interfaces while keeping backward compatibility?

## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to extend behavior without touching core dispatch logic; demonstrates the Command pattern with a runtime registry.

## Key Concepts
- Command pattern
- Registry-based extensibility
- Safe, non-invasive extension

## Code Example
```javascript
interface Command { execute(args: string[]): void; }
class Registry { private map = new Map<string, Command>(); register(name: string, c: Command) { this.map.set(name, c); } get(name: string): Command | undefined { return this.map.get(name); } }
class Router { constructor(private registry: Registry) {} route(argv: string[]) { const cmd = argv[0]; const command = this.registry.get(cmd); if (command) command.execute(argv.slice(1)); else throw new Error('Unknown command'); } }
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you handle asynchronous commands? 
- How would you ensure thread-safety in a multi-process CLI?

## Why This Is Asked
This question probes real-world extensibility: adding rules without redeploying, and per-message routing based on metadata.

## Key Concepts
- Runtime plugin architecture with a Registry
- Minimal Rule interface: apply(Message) -> Result
- Per-message pipeline construction from metadata
- Safe dynamic loading and testing

## Code Example
```javascript
interface Message { region:string; product:string; tier:string; payload:any }
interface Result { ok:boolean; errors?:string[] }
interface Rule { apply(msg:Message):Result }
class Registry { register(name:string, r:Rule):void; get(name:string):Rule | undefined }
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you handle rule versioning and rollback?
- What are the trade-offs of eager vs lazy loading of rules?

## Why This Is Asked
Assess ability to design a modular, runtime-configurable data pipeline with per-tenant customization.

## Key Concepts
- Plugin architecture and the Pipeline pattern
- Immutable pipelines and copy-on-write config
- Per-tenant routing and hot-reload
- Stateless transforms and fault tolerance

## Code Example
```javascript
class Transform { constructor(name){this.name=name} apply(rec){return rec}}
class NormalizeTransform extends Transform { apply(r){ /* normalize */ return r} }
class Pipeline { constructor(steps){ this.steps=Object.freeze(steps)} process(r){ return this.steps.reduce((acc,t)=>t.apply(acc), r) } }
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you test per-tenant hot reload without downtime?
- How do you handle a failing plugin without aborting the whole record?

## Why This Is Asked
This tests the ability to extend behavior without modifying existing code, a hallmark of robust design.

## Key Concepts
- Decorator pattern
- Runtime composition
- Open/Closed Principle

## Code Example
```javascript
interface DataSource { read(): Buffer }
class FileSource implements DataSource { constructor(path) { this.path = path } read() { /* read file */ } }
class DataSourceDecorator implements DataSource { constructor(protected source: DataSource) {} read() { return this.source.read() } }
class EncryptDecorator extends DataSourceDecorator { read() { const data = super.read(); return encrypt(data) } }
class CompressDecorator extends DataSourceDecorator { read() { const data = super.read(); return compress(data) } }
```

- How would you test a decorator stack with different orders?
- How to handle streaming data and backpressure in decorator chains?

## Why This Is Asked

Tests ability to design runtime-extensible routing logic for streaming with low-latency and backpressure concerns. Requires a registry for pluggable policies, a clean, minimal interface, and non-blocking paths to sinks. Examines tradeoffs between hot vs cold storage and observability.

## Key Concepts

- Strategy pattern
- Registry and runtime extensibility
- Backpressure and non-blocking IO
- Hot-swapping configuration

## Code Example

```ts
interface Message { source: string; schema: string; ttl: number }
interface Sink { enqueue(msg: Message): Promise<void> }
interface RoutingPolicy { selectSink(msg: Message): Sink }
class PolicyRegistry { register(name: string, policy: RoutingPolicy): void; get(name: string): RoutingPolicy | undefined }
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you test hot-swapped policies without risking data loss?
- How do you ensure observability across multiple sinks when routing fails?
